questions.to_find: The integer output printed by the C program

understand.plain: We have two functions: g(n) returns n+10, and f(n) returns g(n*2). The main function calls f(f(3)) and prints the result. We need to trace through the nested function calls to find what integer is printed.

keywords.explain: Applying one function to the result of another: f(f(3)) means first compute f(3), then pass that result into f again.
keywords.example: If h(x) = x+1, then h(h(2)) = h(3) = 4.

keywords.explain: In C, function arguments are passed by value — the function receives a copy of the argument's value, not the original variable.
keywords.example: f(3) passes the value 3 to n inside f; changing n inside f does not affect the caller.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>f(3)</text><text x='70' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n=3</text><rect x='160' y='10' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>g(3*2)=g(6)</text><text x='220' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>6+10=16</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>f(16)</text><text x='70' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n=16</text><rect x='160' y='110' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>g(16*2)=g(32)</text><text x='220' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>32+10=42</text><rect x='310' y='110' width='80' height='50' rx='8' class='ok'/><text x='350' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff'>42</text><line x1='130' y1='35' x2='160' y2='35' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='130' y1='135' x2='160' y2='135' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='135' x2='310' y2='135' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='70' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>result=16 passed to f</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call trace: f(3) calls g(6) which returns 16; then f(16) calls g(32) which returns 42 — the final output.

given.aim: Determine the integer printed by printf("%d", f(f(3)))

terms.meaning: Function g adds 10 to its argument.
terms.connects: Called inside f to compute the final result.

terms.meaning: Function f doubles its argument then passes it to g.
terms.connects: The outer and inner call of f(f(3)) both use this definition.

terms.meaning: Nested function call — f applied twice starting from 3.
terms.connects: The argument to printf; we evaluate inside-out.

given.plan: Evaluate inside-out: first compute f(3), then use that result as the argument to the outer f call.

steps.title: Evaluate the inner call f(3)
steps.note: g simply adds 10 to its input; so f(n) = 2n + 10.

steps.title: Evaluate the outer call f(16)
steps.note: The result 16 from step 1 is passed as the argument to the outer f.

steps.title: Determine output
steps.apply: printf prints the integer 42 with no newline. The output is 42.
steps.note: The format specifier %d prints a signed decimal integer.

questions.formula_note: Function composition evaluated inside-out; f(n) = 2n+10 applied twice: f(f(3)) = f(16) = 42.

questions.answer: A. Display[0] = Test AR, Display[1] = A AR, Display[2] = B AR
questions.to_find: The display structure (activation record pointers) when execution is at line z inside procedure B

understand.plain: This question is about runtime stack management using a display structure. A display is an array of pointers where display[i] points to the most recent activation record of the procedure at nesting depth i. We need to trace which activation records are active when we are inside procedure B.

keywords.explain: A block of memory created on the stack each time a procedure is called; it holds local variables, parameters, return address, and saved registers.
keywords.example: When A() is called, an AR for A is pushed on the stack containing variable y.

keywords.explain: An array used for non-local variable access in nested procedures. display[d] points to the activation record of the procedure at lexical (static) nesting depth d.
keywords.example: If Test is at depth 0, A at depth 1, B at depth 2, then display[0]=Test AR, display[1]=A AR, display[2]=B AR.

y1='130' x2='220' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='90' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Stack (bottom to top)</text><text x='285' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Display array</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Stack has Test AR at depth 0, A AR at depth 1, B AR at depth 2. Display[0] points to Test AR, Display[1] to A AR, Display[2] to B AR.

given.aim: Determine the display structure when execution is inside procedure B (at line z), which was called from A, which was called from Test.

terms.meaning: The static (lexical) nesting level of a procedure in the source code.
terms.example: Test is at depth 0, A is nested in Test so depth 1, B is nested in A so depth 2.
terms.connects: The nesting depth d determines which index display[d] the AR is stored at.

terms.meaning: The dynamic order in which procedures were called at runtime.
terms.example: Test calls A; A calls B — so three ARs are on the stack.
terms.connects: All three ARs are live on the stack when we are at line z inside B.

terms.meaning: When procedure at depth d is entered, display[d] is set to point to the new AR; the old value of display[d] is saved in the AR for restoration on return.
terms.example: Entering B (depth 2) sets display[2] = B's AR.
terms.connects: This rule ensures display always holds the most recent AR for each depth.

given.plan: Determine the static nesting depth of each procedure (Test=0, A=1, B=2). When B is executing, the display has one entry per depth for all active procedures: display[0]=Test AR, display[1]=A AR, display[2]=B AR.

steps.title: Determine static nesting depths
steps.apply: Test is the outermost program unit — depth 0. Procedure A is declared inside Test — depth 1. Procedure B is declared inside A — depth 2.
steps.note: Static/lexical depth is determined by the source code nesting, not the call sequence.

steps.title: Identify active activation records at line z
steps.apply: When execution reaches line z inside B: Test's AR is on the stack (depth 0), A's AR is on the stack (depth 1), B's AR is on the stack (depth 2). The display is: display[0] -> Test AR, display[1] -> A AR, display[2] -> B AR.
steps.note: The display allows B to access non-local variables in A (via display[1]) or Test (via display[0]) without traversing the dynamic chain.

solution.result: Display[0] = Test AR, Display[1] = A AR, Display[2] = B AR — option A.

questions.formula_note: Display structure for non-local access: display[d] points to the most recent AR of the procedure at static nesting depth d; updated on each procedure entry and restored on exit.

questions.question:
In which of the following cases is it possible to obtain different results for call-by-reference and call-by-name parameter passing methods?

A. Passing a constant value as a parameter
B. Passing the address of an array as a parameter
C. Passing an array element as a parameter
D. Passing an array as a parameter
questions.answer: C. Passing an array element as a parameter
questions.to_find: The scenario where call-by-reference and call-by-name produce different results

understand.plain: Call-by-reference passes the address of the argument, so any change inside the procedure changes the original variable. Call-by-name re-evaluates the argument expression every time it is used inside the procedure. For most simple arguments these behave the same, but for array elements like a[i], if i changes during the procedure, call-by-name re-evaluates a[i] with the new i, while call-by-reference still refers to the original a[i].

keywords.explain: The address (reference) of the actual argument is passed. The formal parameter acts as an alias for the actual argument.
keywords.example: swap(a, b): inside swap, changes to x directly modify a, and changes to y directly modify b.

keywords.explain: The textual form of the actual argument is substituted for each use of the formal parameter (like a macro expansion). The argument expression is re-evaluated each time the parameter is used.
keywords.example: If procedure P uses param twice and param is a[i], call-by-name evaluates a[i] each time — so if i changes between uses, the two evaluations may yield different array elements.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='190'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:16px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='200' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P(a[i]): i=0 initially, P does i:=i+1 then uses param</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='180' height='70' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Call-by-Reference</text><text x='100' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>param bound to a[0]</text><text x='100' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>always refers to a[0]</text><rect x='210' y='35' width='180' height='70' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Call-by-Name</text><text x='300' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>param re-evaluates a[i]</text><text x='300' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>after i:=i+1, reads a[1]</text><rect x='10' y='125' width='180' height='40' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='100' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>result uses a[0]</text><rect x='210' y='125' width='180' height='40' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='300' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>result uses a[1] -- different!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call-by-reference fixes the reference to a[0] at call time; call-by-name re-evaluates a[i] each use, so if i changes inside the procedure the two methods produce different results.

given.aim: Identify which parameter type causes call-by-reference and call-by-name to yield different outcomes.

terms.meaning: Passing a literal like 5 — there is no address to reference; both methods yield the same value.
terms.example: P(5): call-by-reference is illegal/same; call-by-name always evaluates to 5.
terms.connects: Option A: both methods give same result — no difference.

terms.meaning: Passing a specific element of an array, where the index i is a variable.
terms.example: P(a[i]) with i=0: call-by-ref locks in address of a[0]; call-by-name re-evaluates a[i] on every access.
terms.connects: Option C: if i changes inside P, the two methods access different elements — difference possible.

terms.meaning: Passing the entire array — both call-by-reference and call-by-name typically pass the base address, giving equivalent behaviour.
terms.example: P(arr): both modes give access to the same array memory.
terms.connects: Option D: effectively the same as pass-by-reference; no difference.

given.plan: Analyze each option: for call-by-name, the textual argument is re-evaluated on every use. A difference arises only when the argument expression's value can change between uses — this happens when passing an array element a[i] and i is modified inside the procedure.

steps.title: Analyse options A and B
steps.apply:
A (constant): call-by-name repeatedly evaluates the constant — always same value. Call-by-reference to a constant is typically disallowed or equivalent. No difference.
B (address of array): passing an address is itself a scalar value; both modes pass the same address. No difference.
steps.note: Options A and B cannot produce different results between the two schemes.

steps.title: Analyse option C — array element a[i]
steps.formula_raw: call-by-ref: addr = &a[i] fixed at call; call-by-name: re-evaluate a[i] each use
steps.apply:
Consider P(a[i]) where P has formal parameter x, and inside P it does: i := i + 1; print x.
Call-by-reference: x is bound to &a[i_initial]; after i increases, x still refers to a[i_initial].
Call-by-name: x is textually replaced by a[i]; after i increases, evaluating x gives a[i_new] — a different element.
Result: different values are printed.
steps.note: This is the classic Jensen's device / call-by-name vs call-by-reference distinction for subscripted variables.

steps.title: Analyse option D
steps.apply: Passing an entire array passes the base address in both schemes. No re-evaluation issue arises. No difference.
steps.note: Option D is equivalent to passing a pointer — both schemes behave the same.

solution.result: C. Passing an array element as a parameter — this is where call-by-reference and call-by-name can give different results.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-name re-evaluates the argument expression on every use (textual substitution), unlike call-by-reference which fixes the address at the time of the call; this causes divergence when an array index variable is modified inside the called procedure.
